behaviors.description: Generated function signature matches expected interface (parameter names, types, return type)
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the LLM generates code with incorrect signature — parameter name mismatches, wrong types, or wrong return type. This is fundamentally LLM non-determinism: different prompts or LLM versions generate different signature quality. Mocking the LLM to always return correct signatures would hide the actual failure mode. You must test with the real LLM to catch cases where it misinterprets the spec and generates wrong signatures.
behaviors.test_description: Provide the LLM with a natural language description specifying a function signature (e.g., 'write a function process_data(items: List[str], threshold: float) -> Dict[str, int] that...'). Generate code via the real LLM. Parse the generated code to extract the function signature and compare parameter names, types, and return type against the specification. Run multiple times to account for LLM variance. Assert that signatures match consistently across runs (or document failure rates if variance is high).

behaviors.description: Generated implementation passes all provided test cases on first or subsequent attempt
behaviors.justification: Catching real failures requires executing the generated code against the actual test suite. This demands real code execution, typically in a subprocess or sandbox for safety (executing untrusted LLM-generated code in the main process is risky). Simply checking syntax or using mocks would hide execution failures, type mismatches, and runtime errors. Additionally, to validate that refinement actually improves code quality, you need real test execution with realistic LLM-generated code (Agentic), not mock implementations.
behaviors.test_description: Generate code via the LLM for a target function with a provided test suite. Execute the generated code in a subprocess, running it against each test case. Collect pass/fail results. If tests fail on first attempt, trigger refinement loop and re-execute. Assert that tests eventually pass (by attempt 1, 2, or 3) or that failures are properly captured and reported. Include test cases that are moderately difficult (realistic failure modes), not just trivial cases.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, failure messages are included in the next LLM prompt for refinement
behaviors.justification: This test validates the prompt construction logic — that the system correctly captures test failure output and includes it in the refinement prompt. You can mock both the LLM call and the test execution (return a known failure message) and then intercept/inspect the refinement prompt without needing real LLM calls or real code execution. The failure mode (omitting failure messages) is a straightforward logic bug detectable via integration testing.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return a code sample that will fail tests (e.g., off-by-one error or missing logic). Mock the test execution to return a specific failure message (e.g., 'AssertionError: expected 5, got 4'). Run the service. Intercept the second LLM call (the refinement prompt) and assert that: (1) the failure message appears verbatim in the prompt, (2) the original code is present for context, and (3) the prompt clearly indicates this is a refinement request.

behaviors.description: Service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns structured error with all attempts and results
behaviors.justification: This tests the retry-limit logic: ensuring the service doesn't exceed 3 attempts and properly packages results. You can mock the LLM to always generate code that fails tests, then verify the behavior of the retry counter and error response structure. This is a control-flow bug that doesn't require real code execution or real LLM variance — it's detectable via integration-level mocks.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to always return syntactically valid but logically broken code (e.g., always returns 0, missing branching logic). Mock test execution to always fail with a consistent error message. Run the service on a test task. Assert that exactly 3 generation-test cycles occur (no more, no fewer). Verify the final response is a structured error containing: (1) all 3 generated code samples, (2) all 3 test failure outputs, (3) a clear indication that the limit was reached. Assert no 4th generation attempt is made.

behaviors.description: Full loop produces working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end test of the core value proposition: iterative refinement improves code quality. It requires multiple real agentic steps in sequence (generate → test → refine → test → possibly refine again), executed by the real LLM and real test infrastructure. Mocking the LLM would defeat the purpose — you need the real LLM to demonstrate that: (1) it initially fails on hard functions, and (2) refinement actually helps it improve. This is a multi-step agentic workflow that cannot be validated at lower levels.
behaviors.test_description: Select a moderately complex function that typically fails on a single LLM attempt (e.g., a tricky parsing algorithm, off-by-one counting problem, or complex conditional logic). Provide natural language description and a test suite. Run the full pipeline with the real LLM: generate code, execute tests, capture failures, refine, execute tests again, repeat up to 3 times. Assert that the code passes all tests by attempt 2 or 3. Run this test 5-10 times to verify it works reliably (accounting for LLM variance). Success metric: at least 80% of runs produce passing code by the 3rd attempt.
